title: World War II: military
description: Test your knowledge on America's military victories in World War II!

assessment_items.question_content:
>“ In the future days, which we seek to make secure, we look forward to a world founded upon four essential human freedoms.

> “The first is freedom of speech and expression–everywhere in the world.

> “The second is freedom of every person to worship God in his own way–everywhere in the world

> “The third is freedom from want–which, translated into world terms, means economic understandings which will secure to every nation a healthy peacetime life for its inhabitants — everywhere in the world.

> “The fourth is freedom from fear–which, translated into world terms, means a world-wide reduction of armaments to such a point and in such a thorough fashion that no nation will be in a position to commit an act of physical aggression against any neighbor–anywhere in the world.

> “That is no vision of a distant millennium. It is a definite basis for a kind of world attainable in our own time and generation. That kind of world is the very antithesis of the so-called new order of tyranny which the dictators seek to create with the crash of a bomb.”

>Source: President Franklin D. Roosevelt, State of the Union Address, 1941

**The address excerpted most directly reflected a growing belief after 1941 that:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the United States was fighting against fascism and militarism.
the United States was defending the world from Communism.
the United States was protecting countries from colonization.
the United States forced people in “uncivilized” countries to convert to Christianity.

assessment_items.question_content:
>“ In the future days, which we seek to make secure, we look forward to a world founded upon four essential human freedoms.

> “The first is freedom of speech and expression–everywhere in the world.

> “The second is freedom of every person to worship God in his own way–everywhere in the world

> “The third is freedom from want–which, translated into world terms, means economic understandings which will secure to every nation a healthy peacetime life for its inhabitants — everywhere in the world.

> “The fourth is freedom from fear–which, translated into world terms, means a world-wide reduction of armaments to such a point and in such a thorough fashion that no nation will be in a position to commit an act of physical aggression against any neighbor–anywhere in the world.

> “That is no vision of a distant millennium. It is a definite basis for a kind of world attainable in our own time and generation. That kind of world is the very antithesis of the so-called new order of tyranny which the dictators seek to create with the crash of a bomb.”

>Source: President Franklin D. Roosevelt, State of the Union Address, 1941

**Which of the following most immediately increased enthusiasm in the United States for the ideas outlined in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: reports on wartime atrocities committed by Germany and Japan
increase in the number of refugees from war-torn Europe
news of the Allied victory at the invasion of Normandy
impact of the atomic bombs on Japan, ending World War II

assessment_items.question_content:
>“ In the future days, which we seek to make secure, we look forward to a world founded upon four essential human freedoms.

> “The first is freedom of speech and expression–everywhere in the world.

> “The second is freedom of every person to worship God in his own way–everywhere in the world

> “The third is freedom from want–which, translated into world terms, means economic understandings which will secure to every nation a healthy peacetime life for its inhabitants — everywhere in the world.

> “The fourth is freedom from fear–which, translated into world terms, means a world-wide reduction of armaments to such a point and in such a thorough fashion that no nation will be in a position to commit an act of physical aggression against any neighbor–anywhere in the world.

> “That is no vision of a distant millennium. It is a definite basis for a kind of world attainable in our own time and generation. That kind of world is the very antithesis of the so-called new order of tyranny which the dictators seek to create with the crash of a bomb.”

>Source: President Franklin D. Roosevelt, State of the Union Address, 1941

**Arguments similar to those expressed in the excerpt were later employed to justify which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: intervention in European, Middle Eastern, and Asian countries to stop the spread of Communism
return to isolationism as the threat of fascism and militarism stopped after the defeat of Germany and Japan
creation of welfare and health care programs that aimed to relieve conditions of poverty
introduction of international treaties to limit the production of particularly destructive weapons

assessment_items.question_content:
> “[General Marshall] foresaw a cycle of shortages; that of the moment was the supply shortage, in which the scarcity of supplies hindered mobilization, but he now became convinced that the bottleneck of the future would be that of manpower. He also considered the fact that war had become a complicated business which needed many civilian techniques, and that many of these were almost completely in the control of women. General Marshall asked me why we should try to train men in a specialty such as typing or telephone work which in civilian life has been taken over completely by women; this, he felt, was uneconomical and a waste of time which we didn’t have.” 

>-Source: Interview with Colonel John H. Hilldring, *United States Army in World War II Special Studies The Women's Army Corps,* 1946

**Colonel Hilldring’s remarks in the excerpt most directly reflected which of the following developments during the mid-twentieth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the creation of employment opportunities for women in the military
the rise in women’s participation in factories to support the war effort
the growth of a women’s rights movement focused on equal pay
the lack of highly skilled troops in the United States military

assessment_items.question_content:
> “[General Marshall] foresaw a cycle of shortages; that of the moment was the supply shortage, in which the scarcity of supplies hindered mobilization, but he now became convinced that the bottleneck of the future would be that of manpower. He also considered the fact that war had become a complicated business which needed many civilian techniques, and that many of these were almost completely in the control of women. General Marshall asked me why we should try to train men in a specialty such as typing or telephone work which in civilian life has been taken over completely by women; this, he felt, was uneconomical and a waste of time which we didn’t have.” 

>-Source: Interview with Colonel John H. Hilldring, *United States Army in World War II Special Studies The Women's Army Corps,* 1946

**Which of the following was the most direct effect of conditions such as those described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Women’s socioeconomic situations improved as they earned wages.
Women became eligible to fill combat positions in the military.
Women were replaced with minority troops in these positions
Women’s rights activists organized to protest unequal pay.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “[General Marshall] foresaw a cycle of shortages; that of the moment was the supply shortage, in which the scarcity of supplies hindered mobilization, but he now became convinced that the bottleneck of the future would be that of manpower. He also considered the fact that war had become a complicated business which needed many civilian techniques, and that many of these were almost completely in the control of women. General Marshall asked me why we should try to train men in a specialty such as typing or telephone work which in civilian life has been taken over completely by women; this, he felt, was uneconomical and a waste of time which we didn’t have.” 

>-Source: Interview with Colonel John H. Hilldring, *United States Army in World War II Special Studies The Women's Army Corps,* 1946

**A historian would most likely use this passage to illustrate which of the following? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: how World War II expanded opportunities for women to serve
how racial minorities participated in the war effort
how changes in gender roles led to a women’s rights movement
how the United States military was under-skilled compared to its enemies

assessment_items.question_content:
>“[Critical participation] believes that to fail to protest the wrongs done Negroes as we fight this war is to participate in a crime, not only against Negroes, but against all true anti-Fascists. To fight against defects in our prosecution of the war is regarded as a responsibility. To remain silent simply because friends commit these wrongs is no less dangerous than if Negroes should actively aid the enemy.” 

>-Source: Editor of * The Negro Quarterly,* 1943

**Which of the following most directly contributed to the sentiments expressed in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the widespread segregation across the United States under Jim Crow laws
the migration of African Americans from the South to the North
the growth of a Civil Rights movement that aimed to end voting restrictions
the increase in pro-fascist beliefs on the American homefront during the war

assessment_items.question_content:
>“[Critical participation] believes that to fail to protest the wrongs done Negroes as we fight this war is to participate in a crime, not only against Negroes, but against all true anti-Fascists. To fight against defects in our prosecution of the war is regarded as a responsibility. To remain silent simply because friends commit these wrongs is no less dangerous than if Negroes should actively aid the enemy.” 

>-Source: Editor of * The Negro Quarterly,* 1943

**Arguments similar to those expressed in the excerpt were later employed to justify which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: desegregation of the American military by executive order
use of the conscientious objector status by African Americans
creation of an African American cultural movement in the North
implementation of affirmative action programs to create opportunities

assessment_items.question_content:
>“[Critical participation] believes that to fail to protest the wrongs done Negroes as we fight this war is to participate in a crime, not only against Negroes, but against all true anti-Fascists. To fight against defects in our prosecution of the war is regarded as a responsibility. To remain silent simply because friends commit these wrongs is no less dangerous than if Negroes should actively aid the enemy.” 

>-Source: Editor of * The Negro Quarterly,* 1943

**Based on the excerpt, the author would most likely support which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: desegregating the military
dodging the draft
serving as spies for the Axis powers
ending World War II

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8bbd6d8fde34a5539a90c1f948af9d0a.jpg)

>-Source: Theodor “Dr. Seuss” Geisel, “Well, well! Seems to be a slight shifting of the Japanese current!,” [Special Collections & Archives, UC San Diego](https://library.ucsd.edu/speccoll/dswenttowar/index.html#ark:bb8430928x), 1942

**Which of the following developments is the most direct effect of the situation portrayed in the image?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the start of the United States’ “island-hopping” campaign in the Pacific
the supremacy of Japan’s military in the first half of World War II
the launch of atomic bombs to end Japanese involvement in World War II
the rise in American support for defeating Japan after Pearl Harbor

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8bbd6d8fde34a5539a90c1f948af9d0a.jpg)

>-Source: Theodor “Dr. Seuss” Geisel, “Well, well! Seems to be a slight shifting of the Japanese current!,” [Special Collections & Archives, UC San Diego](https://library.ucsd.edu/speccoll/dswenttowar/index.html#ark:bb8430928x), 1942

**The image was created most directly in response to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: a major air assault by the United States on the Imperial Japanese Navy
a series of US atomic bombs dropped on Hiroshima and Nagasaki
an invasion of Allied troops on the Normandy coastline
an air assault by the Japanese on an American military base

assessment_items.question_content:
![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8bbd6d8fde34a5539a90c1f948af9d0a.jpg)

>-Source: Theodor “Dr. Seuss” Geisel, “Well, well! Seems to be a slight shifting of the Japanese current!,” [Special Collections & Archives, UC San Diego](https://library.ucsd.edu/speccoll/dswenttowar/index.html#ark:bb8430928x), 1942

**The image most strongly supports which of the following arguments about the Battle of Midway?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It was a turning point for the Allies in the Pacific.
It forced the Japanese to surrender to the Allies.
It ended World War II, securing an Allied victory.
It was a stunning defeat for the United States.
